title: W5 B Univariate Differentiation
body:
Mathematical Economics

     Juan Carlos Carbajal

  School of Economics UNSW–Sydney



  Week 5B    —    Term 1, 2026




                                    1/13
 Differentiation in R




                       2/13
                    Derivative of a Function

Definition 1

 The derivative of f : R → R at x is the rate of change of f at x
 for infinitesimally small variations from x:
                                   f (x + t) − f (x)
                   f ′ (x) = lim                     .
                             t→0            t




                                                                    3/13
                        Derivative of a Function

 Definition 1

   The derivative of f : R → R at x is the rate of change of f at x
    for infinitesimally small variations from x:
                                       f (x + t) − f (x)
                       f ′ (x) = lim                     .
                                 t→0            t



An alternative interpretation of the derivative of a function is as a linear
(affine) approximation to the behaviour of f at x.
  ◦ We are interested in approximations to predict changes in the
    behaviour of the function.

  ◦ We focus on linear approximations because the behavior of a linear
    function is simple to analyze.




                                                                               3/13
                 Rate of Change of Affine Functions


Predicting the behavior of a linear or affine function is simple.
  ◦ The rate of change of A(x) = m x + b is
                            △A
                               = m.
                            △x




                                                                    4/13
                 Rate of Change of Affine Functions


Predicting the behavior of a linear or affine function is simple.
  ◦ The rate of change of A(x) = m x + b is
                            △A
                               = m.
                            △x

  ◦ This rate of change is independent of where we measure it.

  ◦ Starting at y ∈ R, the value of A is: A(y) = m y + b.

  ◦ For any t ∈ R, the value of A at (y + t) is: A(y + t) = m (y + t) + b.

               △ A y+t   A(y + t) − A(y)   mt
                       =                 =    = m.
               △x y         y+t − y         t




                                                                             4/13
             Rates of Change of Non-Linear Functions



Let f (x) = 5 − (2 − x)2 , for x ∈ R.

Claim The rate of change of f is not constant.
  ◦ Starting at y ∈ R, the value of f is: f (y) = 5 − (2 − y)2 .

  ◦ For any t ∈ R, the value of f at (y + t) is: f (y + t) = 5 − (2 − y − t)2 .

  ◦ The rate of change is

                      △ f y+t   −(2 − y − t)2 + (2 − y)2
                              =
                      △x y                   t
                                2 (2 − y) t − t2
                              =                  .
                                        t




                                                                                  5/13
               Derivatives as Affine Approximations


Our goal is to approximate the behavior of f around x using simple rates
of change. To accomplish it, use affine function that mimics f around x.
  ◦ Basic requirement: A(x) = f (x).

  ◦ But there are many affine functions that satisfy this.


                                     A1
                                                         f
                                               A3


                                          A2

                                 x                   x




                                                                           6/13
               Derivatives as Affine Approximations

Our goal is to approximate the behavior of f around x using simple rates
of change. To accomplish it, use affine function that mimics f around x.
  ◦ To find the best approximation, define an affine function

               A(u) = m u + (f (x) − m x),         for u ∈ R.

  ◦ Note that A(x) = f (x) as desired.

  ◦ Note also that A(u) − A(x) = m (u − x) = T (u − x).

  ◦ To find the best affine approximation we would require that the
    error |A(u) − f (u)| → 0 faster than |u − x| → 0.

  ◦ This is equivalent to requiring that
                                               
                     A(u) − A(x) − f (u) − f (x) → 0

    faster than |u − x| → 0.



                                                                           7/13
             Derivatives as Linear Approximations




◦ Since | − z| = |z|, we can write the above requirement as
                                         
    f (u) − f (x)       −   A(u) − A(x)       = |f (u) − f (x) − T (u − x)| .

◦ To find the best linear approximation, verify that the expression

                             |f (u) − f (x) − T (u − x)|
                                        |u − x|

  converges to 0 as u → x.




                                                                                8/13
            Derivatives as Linear Approximations



Definition 2

 Let I be an open interval and f : I → R. Say that f is differentiable
 at x ∈ I if there is a linear mapping T : R → R such that
                      f (u) − f (x) − T (u − x)
                lim                               = 0.
                u→x              u−x

 In this case, the linear mapping T is called the derivative of f at x
 and denoted by T = Df (x).




                                                                         9/13
              Derivatives as Linear Approximations



 Definition 2

   Let I be an open interval and f : I → R. Say that f is differentiable
   at x ∈ I if there is a linear mapping T : R → R such that
                        f (u) − f (x) − T (u − x)
                  lim                               = 0.
                  u→x              u−x

   In this case, the linear mapping T is called the derivative of f at x
   and denoted by T = Df (x).


Observe Definition 2 is equivalent to

                        f (u) − f (x)
                  lim                 = Df (x).
                  u→x       u−x




                                                                           9/13
                                Examples




Example 1

 The derivative of a constant function c(x) = κ is Dc(x) = 0:
         |c(u) − c(x) − T (u − x)|       |c(u) − c(x) − Dc(x)(u − x)|
   lim                             = lim
   u→x            |u − x|            u→x            |u − x|




                                                                        10/13
                                Examples




Example 1

 The derivative of a constant function c(x) = κ is Dc(x) = 0:
         |c(u) − c(x) − T (u − x)|       |c(u) − c(x) − Dc(x)(u − x)|
   lim                             = lim
   u→x            |u − x|            u→x            |u − x|

                                            |κ − κ − 0 (u − x)|
                                   = lim
                                      u→x        |u − x|

                                            |κ − κ|
                                   = lim              = 0.
                                      u→x |u − x|




                                                                        10/13
                                 Examples




Example 2

 The derivative of f (x) = x is Df (x) = 1:
         |f (u) − f (x) − T (u − x)|       |f (u) − f (x) − Df (x)(u − x)
   lim                               = lim
  u→x              |u − x|             u→x              u − x
                                              |u − x − 1 (u − x)|
                                    = lim
                                       u→x         |u − x|

                                    = 0.




                                                                            11/13
                                Examples


Example 3

 The derivative of g(x) = x2 is Dg(x) = 2x:
         |g(u) − g(x) − T (u − x)|       |g(u) − g(x) − Dg(x)(u − x)
   lim                             = lim
   u→x            |u − x|            u→x            u − x




                                                                       12/13
                                Examples


Example 3

 The derivative of g(x) = x2 is Dg(x) = 2x:
         |g(u) − g(x) − T (u − x)|       |g(u) − g(x) − Dg(x)(u − x)
   lim                             = lim
   u→x            |u − x|            u→x            u − x

                                           |u2 − x2 − 2x (u − x)|
                                  = lim
                                     u→x          |u − x|

                                           |u2 − 2u x + x2 |
                                  = lim
                                     u→x        |u − x|




                                                                       12/13
                                Examples


Example 3

 The derivative of g(x) = x2 is Dg(x) = 2x:
         |g(u) − g(x) − T (u − x)|       |g(u) − g(x) − Dg(x)(u − x)
   lim                             = lim
   u→x            |u − x|            u→x            u − x

                                           |u2 − x2 − 2x (u − x)|
                                  = lim
                                     u→x          |u − x|

                                           |u2 − 2u x + x2 |
                                  = lim
                                     u→x        |u − x|

                                              |u − x|2
                                  = lim                = 0.
                                     u→x       |u − x|




                                                                       12/13
                                 Examples


Example 3

 The derivative of g(x) = x2 is Dg(x) = 2x:
         |g(u) − g(x) − T (u − x)|       |g(u) − g(x) − Dg(x)(u − x)
   lim                             = lim
   u→x            |u − x|            u→x            u − x

                                            |u2 − x2 − 2x (u − x)|
                                   = lim
                                      u→x          |u − x|

                                            |u2 − 2u x + x2 |
                                   = lim
                                      u→x        |u − x|

                                              |u − x|2
                                   = lim               = 0.
                                      u→x      |u − x|

 Using an induction argument, one can show that the derivative of f (x) = xn
 is Df (x) = nxn−1 .




                                                                               12/13
             Derivatives as Linear Approximations


Example 4

 The derivative of f (x) = 5 − (2 − x)2 at y ∈ R is Df (y) = 4 − 2y.




                                                                       13/13
                Derivatives as Linear Approximations


 Example 4

   The derivative of f (x) = 5 − (2 − x)2 at y ∈ R is Df (y) = 4 − 2y.


Show Following the definition of the derivative of f at y obtains

               f (y + t) − f (y)       −(2 − y − t)2 + (2 − y)2
         lim                     = lim
         t→0            t          t→0             t
                                         2 (2 − y) t − t2
                               = lim
                                   t→0           t




                                                                         13/13
                Derivatives as Linear Approximations


 Example 4

   The derivative of f (x) = 5 − (2 − x)2 at y ∈ R is Df (y) = 4 − 2y.


Show Following the definition of the derivative of f at y obtains

               f (y + t) − f (y)       −(2 − y − t)2 + (2 − y)2
         lim                     = lim
         t→0            t          t→0             t
                                     2 (2 − y) t − t2
                               = lim
                                   t→0       t
                                                   
                               = lim 2 (2 − y) − t = 4 − 2y.
                                   t→0




                                                                         13/13
